6. Adiabatic Approximation

6.1. Adiabatic Changes and Invariants

Definition 6. Consider some parameter in Hamiltonian with explicit time dependence. The change in time of these is called adiabatic if it is slow compred with the “natural” timescale of the system. In particular, we want

(71)

Often the natural timescale itself changes, so if we set we get an equivalent condition

(72)

This condition looks similar to the WKB spatial condition . A third equivalent condition will be derived in the proof for the adiabatic theorem (see iPad):

(73)

Suppose changes by from the interval to . Increasing while keeping fixed, slows down the rate οf change of . Let be a quantity constructed by and other parameters of the system.

itself is not an adiabatic invariant since . We will explore the SHO under this framework.

Can we find an adiabatic invariant?

This turns out to be a gauge transform with the Berry connection.

6.2. Formalism

The adiabatic condition yields and . See iPad notes. We motivate being a classical adiabatic invariant. An interested geometric consequence is that the enclosed area in phase space is invariant, which holds more generally for closed trajectories. In quantum physics, that implies is an adiabatic invariant. We showed how to motivate this with the WKB approximation and first order TDPT.

Theorem 6 (Adiabatic Theorem). Let be a continuously varying Hamiltonian for . Let the state of the system at be one of the instantaneous eigenstates for some . Then at any time we have

(80)

up to a calculable phase. The amplitude to transition to any other instantaneous eigenstate is of order . This implies that the probability of remaining in the instantaneous eigenstate equals unity, up to corrections of order .

Here, transitions refer to between instantaeous energy eigenstates, i.e. jumping between energy levels. A natural timescale might be

where all the variables are time-dependent. Assume for a moment that the mixing term is zero. Then the evolution of is proportional to and this implies the adiabatic theorem:

(82)

We write this as

(83)

where and are the dynamical phase and Berry phase. The Berry phase is a phase and not an exponential decay becasue the quantity is purely imaginary (Proposition 10.)

We’d like to show the mixing term creates a small contribution when integrated. Assuming the spectra is nondegenerate, We can compute

(84)

and we expect the mixing term to have a small total contribution when Let’s check this. In general write The goal is to show the time-dependent residual vanishes. 6.3. Application: Landau-Zener Transitions

You excite a NaCl molecule with a laser to a dissociative state. The molecule breaks apart, and you analyze the fragments. The photon energy determines how far up the potential energy surface you reach, i.e., what internuclear distances RR are sampled during dissociation. If the photon energy is tuned to near the avoided crossing between ionic and neutral states, nonadiabatic transitions (governed by Landau-Zener) can occur.

Example: At specific photon energies, you might observe more neutral fragments (Na + Cl) than ionic (Na⁺ + Cl⁻), indicating a transition between surfaces.

Let atomic and ionic states be our two state system.

Adiabatic: Energy levels don’t cross (adiabatic theorem) but state (instantaeous eigenstate) changes due to atomic bonds being higer and lower energy at shroter and larger distances. See 4/17 lecture for explanation.

Diabatic: Energy levels can cross and the state remains the same.

[TODO] quantify probability of transition. For a fast sweep we should get and for a slow sweep we should get 1.

6.4. Berry’s Phase

Proposition 10. The Berry phase is a phase and not an exponential decay becasue the quantity is purely imaginary
Proof

Take the time derivative of .

□

The Berry phase has a geometric meaning. Let the independent parameters of the system be . Then, the time evolution can be described as a path in -space. Using our formula for Berry’s phase yields a path integral in -space that is independent of time. The path integral is

(86)

where is the Berry connection, defined below.

Definition 8. The Berry connection for the th instantaneous eigenstate is

(87)

Note that is over parameter space, not over any metric space, so if our parameters our spherical coordinates then we don’t use the usual del in spherical coordinates formula. This is analogous to the vector potential in electrodynamics and has gauge transforms.

Proposition 11. Berry’s phase is gauge invariant if is a closed path.
Proof

Gauge transforms add the gradient of a function.

□

Proposition 12.

  1. If the instantaneous eigenstates are real, Berry’s phase vanishes.
  2. If is one-dimensional, Berry’s phase vanishes for any closed loop .
  3. In 3D space, the vector potential, or connection , has an associated magnetic field , sometimes called the curvature of the onnection. We can do the same for Berry’s connection, associating Berry’s curvature
Proof
  1. Follows from Proposition 10. as must be purely imaginary. Can also explicitly compute using integral.
  2. This is a trivial property of one dimensional path integrals.
  3. Define .

□

The phase of a single state is not an observable but difference in Berry’s phases is2. Consider a state in superposition of two instantaneous eigenstates. Then each of the two eigenstates evolves and each will have a Berry phase. We can measure their difference in phase using interferometry ([TODO] how does this work.).

Example 5 (Berry phase for electron in a slowly varying magnetic field). The magnetic field is described by . Suppose we start out at and magnetic field vector traces out a closed loop. We can compute the Berry phase to be

(88)

This is independent of the change in .

6.4.1. What’s the point of Berrys’ phase?

The dynamical phase is sensitive to the path of and how fast changes at each point. In contrast, Berry’s phase depends only on the path takes through parameter space and introduces a topological component to the acquired wavefunction phase.

6.5. Born-Oppenheimer Approximation

For large separations, we have attractive Van Der Waals force (see 8.06 midterm). For short separations we have Coulomb repulsion, so we expect vibrtaions about an equilibrium configuration. The brute force solution of a molecule is extremely complcated. We can treat the slow motion of nuclei relative to electrons as adiabatic.

Proposition 13. In a diatomic molecule with electrons of mass and nuclear (reduced) mass .

(89)

Proof

Suppose the molecule has size , which is a few multiples of . We have

(90)

  • Write . The vibration is entirely electronic in nature, and so should not directly depend on mass. Displacing the nuclei by a distance of changes the electronic energys by a factor of , and hence we have .
  • For rotations, say and .

□

Zwiebach uses as the slowly changing variable and computes an effective Hamiltonian that is the nuclear Hamiltonian3 with the electron energy set to its avergae as a function of . He completely omits tensor notation.

TLDR Oversimplifcation: Effectively you can write eigenstates as and the Schrodinger equation becomes , i.e. electrons remain in the same instantaneous eigenstate?
